title: Markets
summary: 9609 AS — market types, size, growth, competition, and physical vs online markets.
learningObjectives: Define a market and distinguish local, national, global markets.
Explain market size and growth measurement.
Evaluate opportunities in physical vs online markets.

simpleExplanation.title: Where exchange happens
simpleExplanation.summary: A market is where buyers and sellers meet — physically or digitally. Size, growth, and competition shape marketing strategy.
steps: Market size measured by value or volume.
Concentrated vs fragmented competition changes strategy.

flashcards.back: Buyers and sellers of a particular product or service — not necessarily a physical place.
Total value (£) or volume (units) of sales in a period.
Rate of increase in market size — attracts investment and new competitors.
Niche: small specialised segment. Mass: large broad market (links 3.1.5).
Lower entry barriers, global reach, data on customers.
Intense price competition, delivery costs, trust and returns.
Few large firms dominate — heavy branding and non-price competition.
External factors (technology, economic) reshape market size and growth.

sections.content: **Markets** (3.1.3) sets context for **segmentation** and **mix** decisions. Case questions often give **market growth %** and **number of competitors** — use them in recommend answers.

**Size** — total revenue or units; indicates opportunity.

**Growth** — expanding markets favour investment; declining markets favour consolidation or exit.

**Competition** — many small rivals (fragmented) vs few dominant firms (oligopoly) affects pricing power.

**Geography** — local, national, export, global online.

sections.question: UK plant-based milk market: £400 m sales, growing 12% annually, dominated by 3 brands with 70% share. A start-up enters. Discuss two market features that matter.
sections.solution:
**Growth (12%):** Attractive for entry — rising tide lifts demand; room for new products without stealing static share.

**Concentration (3 brands, 70%):** **High barriers** — incumbents have shelf space, brand trust, marketing budgets. Start-up needs **niche positioning** (3.1.5) or **innovation** rather than head-on mass marketing.
